metadata.summary: Split Markdown by header hierarchy and HTML by sectioning elements, injecting parent header paths into each chunk to preserve document context for retrieval.

content.title: Document Structure Chunking (Markdown and HTML)
content.summary: MarkdownChunker splits documents at header boundaries while propagating parent headers into child chunks as a header_path. HTMLChunker uses BeautifulSoup to split by section/article/div elements. Both strategies preserve navigational context inside every chunk, improving embedding quality and retrieval traceability.

why.p: Markdown and HTML documents organize information hierarchically. Splitting them by character count discards the hierarchy: a chunk that says "The method returns None" without its enclosing section header "Error Handling" loses the context a retriever needs to decide relevance. MarkdownChunker captures that context by inheriting parent headers and embedding their titles inside each chunk text.

HTML sectioning elements (section, article, div with headings) carry the same structural signal. HTMLChunker uses BeautifulSoup to walk the DOM rather than text patterns, handling attributes, nested elements, and script/style tags correctly.

list.item: Processing Markdown documentation sites, wikis, or README files where header hierarchy must be preserved.
Indexing HTML pages that use semantic sectioning elements (section, article).
Any corpus where retrieval queries reference section context ("what does the authentication section say about tokens?").
Documentation RAG pipelines for LLM tool/API references, where a function's section parent provides critical context.
Flat HTML pages with no sectioning elements — HTMLChunker produces one giant chunk because there are no div/section/article boundaries.
Code files — use chunking-code-ast; MarkdownChunker can produce very small chunks on single-line code blocks that embed poorly.
Unstructured prose (news, essays) without headers — use chunking-semantic or RecursiveChunker instead.
MarkdownChunker without min_chunk_size guard on documentation with many shallow headers — single-line list items produce near-zero vectors that pollute nearest-neighbor results.

rule.statement: MarkdownChunker MUST set include_header_in_chunk=True by default. Every produced chunk MUST carry a header_path field that is the concatenation of all ancestor header titles joined by " > ". When a content block exceeds max_chunk_size, MarkdownChunker MUST split it and carry the same header_path into each sub-chunk.
rule.rationale: Embedding the header path inside the chunk text improves retrieval recall for queries that reference section context. Without header_path propagation, sub-chunks of large sections lose their navigational anchor and become indistinguishable from each other in embedding space.

example.title: MarkdownChunker with header path propagation
example.note: header_path can exceed 200 chars for deeply nested docs. Verify against vector DB metadata field limits before indexing.

antipattern.description: Running MarkdownChunker without a min_chunk_size guard on documentation heavy with one-line code blocks or list items.
antipattern.reason: MarkdownChunker can produce very small chunks (single-line code blocks, list items) that embed poorly. min_chunk_size guard is essential — empty code blocks and single-word paragraphs produce near-zero vectors that pollute nearest-neighbor results.

rule.statement: HTMLChunker MUST use BeautifulSoup with find_all(['section', 'article', 'div']) as the primary splitting surface. For each element, it MUST extract the nearest heading (h1-h6) as the chunk header. Elements exceeding max_chunk_size MUST be word-split into numbered parts carrying the same header. The caller MUST pass well-formed HTML; malformed input behavior is undefined.
rule.rationale: Flat text splitting of HTML includes tag noise and misses structural boundaries. BeautifulSoup's DOM walk respects nesting and script/style tags. Numbered parts preserve source traceability when a large section splits into multiple chunks.

example.title: HTMLChunker using BeautifulSoup
example.note: Flat HTML pages with no section/article/div elements produce one giant chunk — validate input has structural elements before using HTMLChunker.

antipattern.description: Using HTMLChunker on flat HTML pages (marketing landing pages, email templates) that have no semantic sectioning elements.
antipattern.reason: HTMLChunker targets section/article/div; flat HTML pages with no sectioning elements produce one giant chunk. Pre-check structure with soup.find('section') or soup.find('article') before choosing this chunker.

list.item: beautifulsoup4 — HTML parsing for HTMLChunker: `pip install beautifulsoup4`
Unstructured.io — pre-processes PDFs/DOCX into clean structural elements before MarkdownChunker
LlamaParse (SaaS) — table-aware PDF parsing; critical before MarkdownChunker on academic/legal PDFs
Docling (IBM OSS) — local, preserves document layout hierarchy; good for enterprise air-gap deployments

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: MarkdownChunker inherits parent headers into sub-chunks — this is intentional but can cause header_path to be more than 200 chars for deeply nested docs, which breaks some vector DB metadata limits. Truncate or hash header_path before storing in the index.

When MarkdownChunker is used with include_header_in_chunk=True, the header line appears in the chunk text, so embedding the chunk naturally includes section context without an extra metadata field lookup at query time.

checklist.check: Set include_header_in_chunk=True — embedding header path inside chunk text improves retrieval recall.
Guard against header_path exceeding vector DB metadata limits (commonly 200 chars) for deeply nested documents.
Pre-validate HTML input has sectioning elements before applying HTMLChunker.
Apply a min_chunk_size filter after chunking to discard near-empty chunks from single-line code blocks or list items.
Verify chunk size distribution: log warning if more than 5% of chunks exceed max_chunk_size.
Use LlamaParse or Docling to pre-process PDFs before applying MarkdownChunker — raw PDF text lacks structural markers.
